label: Local Home School
description: Attend your designated neighbourhood school — guaranteed enrollment, no application required, and all four OSSD pathways available to you.
details: Every Toronto student has a designated home school within either the TDSB (Toronto District School Board) or the TCDSB (Toronto Catholic District School Board), based on their home address and religious preference. Attending your local school means guaranteed enrollment, a shorter commute across the city, and staying connected with your elementary school friends. Toronto's neighbourhood high schools offer the complete Ontario curriculum across all destination pathways (U, C, E, O), a wide range of electives, sports teams, clubs, co-op placements, and often SHSM (Specialist High Skills Major) programs. Don't underestimate your local school — many of Toronto's top university graduates started right in their neighbourhood school.
pros: No application required — your spot is guaranteed
Shorter commute across the city means more time and less stress
Stay connected with existing friends from elementary school
Full range of OSSD courses (all pathways) available
Clubs, sports teams, co-op, and SHSM programs available
cons: May not offer the specific specialty program you're passionate about
Peer group is determined by geography, not shared interests
Less exposure to students from across Toronto's diverse neighbourhoods
applicationProcess: None required. You are automatically enrolled at your designated home school. Use the TDSB School Locator or the TCDSB school registration page to find your designated school. For TDSB, contact your local school directly to confirm registration. For TCDSB: non-Catholic students are welcome to enroll in TCDSB secondary schools — you do not need to be Catholic to attend. A baptismal certificate is only required for Catholic students directing their tax support to the Catholic board.
whoItsFor: Students who value proximity, social continuity, or flexibility — or who haven't yet identified a specific program passion.
keyFeatures: All destination codes: U, C, E, O
admissionAvg: No academic requirement — guaranteed enrollment for all students in the catchment area. TDSB and TCDSB each have their own catchment boundaries.

label: Specialized Program
description: Apply to a focused program at a specific Toronto school — arts, tech, IB, French Immersion, or elite sports — designed for students with a strong passion for one area.
details: Both TDSB and TCDSB offer a wide range of specialized programs at designated schools across Toronto. Students travel from across the city to attend, forming a community of like-minded peers. TDSB programs include: Etobicoke School of the Arts, Claude Watson School for the Arts, Rosedale Heights School of the Arts, Marc Garneau Collegiate (AP/STEM), International Baccalaureate at several schools, Extended French and French Immersion programs, and elite sports academies. TCDSB offers Cardinal Carter Academy for the Arts, Michael Power/St. Joseph High School (arts), Neil McNeil High School (IB), and French Immersion programs at several Catholic schools. Applications are managed through each board's own portal and typically open in November–December.
pros: Deep immersion in your area of passion from Day 1
Peers who share your interests and drive from across Toronto
Programs often not available at neighbourhood schools
May provide university/college portfolio or credential advantage
Often leads to stronger sense of belonging and identity
cons: Longer TTC or transit commute across the city
May mean leaving your elementary school friend group
High expectations and workload in your focus area
applicationProcess: TDSB: Apply through the TDSB Specialized Programs application portal at tdsb.on.ca. TCDSB: Apply through the TCDSB Specialized and Alternative Programs portal at tcdsb.org. Depending on the program: submit a portfolio (arts), attend an audition (music/drama/dance), write an academic assessment (IB), or submit reference letters. Most TDSB deadlines fall in January; TCDSB deadlines are typically in December–January. Check each board's website for exact dates.
whoItsFor: Students with a clear passion for a specific area who are ready to commit to focused study and are willing to audition or apply.
keyFeatures: Focused curriculum in your specialty area
Cross-city peer community of shared interest
Portfolio, audition, or assessment admission process
TDSB and TCDSB each have their own application portals
admissionAvg: Varies by program. Arts programs require auditions/portfolios. IB programs assess academic readiness (typically 75%+). Apply in Nov–Jan of Grade 8 through your board's portal.
interests: Deep passion for arts, tech, sports, or languages
Meeting peers who share your specific interest
Being challenged in your area of strength
Willing to commute across Toronto for the right opportunity

label: Out of Area Transfer
description: Request to attend a Toronto school outside your designated catchment area — for a sibling connection, a specific elective, a favourite team, or a school culture that fits you better.
details: Both TDSB and TCDSB allow students to apply for an out-of-area (boundary) transfer to attend a school that isn't their designated home school. This is different from a specialized program — you're requesting to attend a regular school in a different Toronto neighbourhood. Common reasons: a sibling already attends that school, the school is geographically closer to a parent/guardian's workplace, a specific sport team, elective, or club is only available there, or the school's community and culture feels like a better personal fit. TDSB refers to this as a 'non-resident' or 'discretionary' placement. TCDSB handles transfers through their Student Placement office. Approval depends on space availability at the receiving school.
pros: Access a Toronto school that better fits your situation
Follow a sibling, close friend, or existing connection
May be closer to a parent/guardian's workplace in the city
Access a specific team, club, or elective not at your home school
cons: Not guaranteed — subject to available space at the receiving school
Requires a formal TDSB or TCDSB application and approval
You may still be required to use your home school if space is unavailable
TTC or transit costs are usually your responsibility
applicationProcess: TDSB: Submit a non-resident or discretionary placement request through your local TDSB school or the TDSB Student Support Services office. TCDSB: Contact the TCDSB Student Placement office (tcdsb.org) to request a transfer. Include a clear reason for your request — sibling connection, workplace proximity, or specific program. Apply between January and March. Approval is space-dependent.
whoItsFor: Students who want a different regular Toronto school experience without a specialized program — due to family connections, location, or school culture preference.
keyFeatures: Regular full OSSD curriculum at a different Toronto school
Access to that school's unique electives, teams, and clubs
TDSB or TCDSB managed application process
Same courses and pathways as any Toronto school
admissionAvg: No academic threshold — based on available space and board policy. Apply January–March of Grade 8 through your TDSB or TCDSB board office.
interests: Sibling or family connection to a specific Toronto school
School closer to a parent's or guardian's workplace in the city
Specific sports team, club, or elective only at that school
School community or culture that feels like a better fit

label: Gifted / Enrichment Program
description: If formally identified as 'Gifted' through Ontario's IPRC process, access a congregated gifted class within TDSB or TCDSB — an enriched environment built for abstract thinking, depth, and intellectual challenge.
details: Both TDSB and TCDSB offer Gifted programs for students formally identified through the Identification, Placement and Review Committee (IPRC) process, which involves a psycho-educational assessment (typically including an IQ test). In TDSB, congregated Gifted classes are offered at designated secondary schools across the city — students are placed at a school with a gifted cohort based on their home area. TCDSB offers enrichment and gifted support through its Special Education department. Congregated Gifted classes bring identified students together for full-time enrichment: accelerated curriculum, Socratic discussion, independent research, and an Individual Education Plan (IEP). Speak to your elementary school principal in September of Grade 8 to start the referral process.
pros: Intellectual peer group who think at a similar level
IEP provides individual support and accommodations
Often includes independent research projects and competitions
Strong preparation for competitive university programs
cons: Requires formal IPRC identification — not everyone qualifies
May mean attending a specific TDSB or TCDSB school outside your catchment
Social adjustment — leaving current friend group
applicationProcess: Speak to your elementary school's principal or vice-principal about starting the IPRC (Identification, Placement and Review Committee) referral. Both TDSB and TCDSB manage this through their Special Education departments. A psycho-educational assessment is required and typically takes 2–3 months. Request the referral in September–October of Grade 8 to meet secondary placement deadlines. Contact TDSB Special Education at tdsb.on.ca or TCDSB Special Education at tcdsb.org.
whoItsFor: Students who have already been identified as gifted, OR students whose parents or teachers believe they may qualify and want to pursue formal IPRC assessment through TDSB or TCDSB.
keyFeatures: May include: Advanced Placement (AP), math/science competitions, debate
Formal IPRC identification required — managed by TDSB or TCDSB Special Education
admissionAvg: Formal IPRC identification required. Typically involves a full psycho-educational assessment. Contact TDSB Special Education (tdsb.on.ca) or TCDSB Special Education (tcdsb.org).
interests: Academic challenge that goes beyond the regular classroom
Complex ideas, big questions, and abstract thinking
Intellectual peers who love debating and analysing
